title: Repairing a small tear in leather
subtitle: Back the tear, bond the flaps, flatten and tint
excerpt: How to repair a clean or ragged tear in leather upholstery: trimming ragged edges, inserting a backing patch, bonding the flaps with leather contact adhesive, pressing flat, and touching up the colour.
sourceNotes: Leatherwork repair practice. City & Guilds Leatherwork Level 2. Al Stohlman The Art of Hand Sewing Leather (1977). Conservation practice notes from English Heritage and the Leather Conservation Centre.

content.text: A tear in leather upholstery can be repaired invisibly if the flaps are still present and the grain surface is intact. The method is to glue a backing patch under the tear to hold the two sides aligned, bond the flaps down from underneath, then tint the surface to match. A clean cut repairs more reliably than a ragged tear, but both follow the same method.
Preparing the tear
Step 1, trim ragged edges.

If the tear has ragged or lifted edges, trim them with a sharp knife. Ragged edges create ridges under the finished repair. For a clean straight tear, skip trimming entirely. Do not remove more material than necessary; keep the flaps as large as possible for a full-width bond.
Step 2, clean the area.

Wipe the tear and the 20 mm surrounding it with a cloth dampened lightly in isopropyl alcohol. Remove any wax, silicone, or conditioner from the surface; leather adhesive will not bond through a conditioned or waxed surface. Allow to dry fully before proceeding.
Backing and bonding
Step 3, cut a backing patch.

Cut a scrap of thin flexible fabric (cotton or thin leather scrap) roughly 20 mm larger than the tear in each direction. This patch goes underneath the tear to hold the two sides in alignment. If the item is upholstered, the patch is inserted through the tear opening and positioned by feel.
Step 4, glue the patch in place.

Apply leather contact adhesive to the top surface of the patch and to the underside of the leather flaps on both sides of the tear. Leather contact adhesive is applied to both mating surfaces, allowed to go touch-dry for 3 to 5 minutes, then pressed together. Slide the patch into position under the tear. Press the leather flaps down onto the patch with firm thumb pressure, holding for 30 seconds.
Step 5, close the surface.

Apply a thin line of leather adhesive into the tear itself using a fine brush or cocktail stick. Press the two edges of the tear together and hold flat for one minute. Weight the repair under a flat board for an hour while the adhesive sets fully.
Finishing
Step 6, tint the repair.

Apply a leather colourant matched to the surrounding leather colour over the repair line. Use a small sponge and build to opacity in thin coats, allowing each to dry for 10 minutes before applying the next. Blend outward from the repair into the surrounding leather. Seal with a leather finish spray when the colour matches.
Common mistakes
Repair lifting at the edges after a few weeks.
The surface was not cleaned of conditioner or wax before gluing. Strip the area again with isopropyl alcohol and re-bond.
Visible ridge along the tear line.
The flaps were not trimmed flat or the backing patch is too thick. Sand the ridge very lightly with 400-grit paper before tinting, working with the grain direction only.
